---
author: Tristan Muno
date: last-modified
date-format: MMMM D, YYYY
bibliography: ../references.bib
biblio-style: apsr
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    preview-links: false
    slideNumber: true
    incremental: true
    code-fold: show
    code-summary: "View source"
    pdfSeparateFragments: true
    footer: QMIR -- {{< meta date >}} -- Week 5
    logo: ./images/uma_ss.png
    theme: theme.scss
editor: source
execute:
  echo: true
  message: false
  warning: false
  freeze: true
---

## QMIR 2026

### Week 5: Reshaping, Merging & Data Visualization

![](./images/uma_palace.png){width="100%"} {{< meta author >}} <a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H2a2 2 0 0 1-2-2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a> <br> {{< meta date >}}

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| eval: true
#| warning: false

# Load all packages used in this deck
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(janitor)
library(knitr)
library(here)

# ── Small demo tibbles for join slides ──────────────────────────────────────
x <- tibble(country = c("Germany", "France", "Poland"), ghg = c(9.3, 5.7, 9.4))
y <- tibble(country = c("Germany", "France", "Spain"), gdp = c(117, 103, 86))

# ── Eurostat data (full pipeline) ────────────────────────────────────────────
ghg <- read_excel(
  here(
    "slides",
    "data",
    "Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx"
  ),
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  skip = 9
) |>
  clean_names() |>
  rename(country = time) |>
  filter(!is.na(country), !country %in% c("Special value", ":")) |>
  select(-x2019) |>
  pivot_longer(-country, names_to = "year", values_to = "ghg_pc") |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(str_remove(year, "^x")))

gdp <- read_excel(
  here(
    "slides",
    "data",
    "Eurostat_GDPpc.xlsx"
  ),
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  skip = 8
) |>
  clean_names() |>
  rename(country = time) |>
  filter(!is.na(country)) |>
  mutate(x2020 = as.numeric(x2020)) |>
  pivot_longer(-country, names_to = "year", values_to = "gdp_pc") |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(str_remove(year, "^x")))

eu_data <- left_join(ghg, gdp, by = c("country", "year")) |>
  mutate(
    country = fct(country),
    country = fct_recode(
      country,
      "EU27" = "European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)"
    )
  )
```

# Housekeeping & Workflow Tips

---

## Positron Keyboard Shortcuts {.smaller}

Knowing these will make your workflow noticeably faster.

| Action | Windows/Linux | Mac |
|---|---|---|
| **Run current line / selection** | `Ctrl + Enter` | `Cmd + Enter` |
| **Run entire cell** | `Ctrl + Shift + Enter` | `Cmd + Shift + Enter` |
| **Comment / uncomment code** | `Ctrl + /` | `Cmd + /` |
| **Duplicate line** | `Shift + Alt + ↓` | `Shift + Option + ↓` |
| **Select current word** | `Ctrl + D` | `Cmd + D` |
| **Open/close Explorer panel** | `Ctrl + Shift + E` | `Cmd + Shift + E` |
| **Open/close Terminal** | `` Ctrl + ` `` | `` Cmd + ` `` |
| **Restart R session** | `Ctrl + Shift + F10` | `Cmd + Shift + F10` |
| **Insert code cell** | `Ctrl + Alt + I` | `Cmd + Option + I` |

::: {.callout-tip}
**Restart R frequently.** A fresh session catches hidden dependencies between code chunks that can break rendering.
:::

---

## The `package::function` Notation {.smaller}

You will encounter this pattern often in documentation and Stack Overflow answers:

```r
dplyr::filter(gapminder, year == 2007)
```

. . .

This is equivalent to loading the package and then calling the function:

```r
library(dplyr)
filter(gapminder, year == 2007)
```

. . .

**When to use it:**

- When two packages export a function with the **same name** (e.g., `dplyr::filter` vs `stats::filter`)
- In scripts you share with others, to make dependencies explicit
- When you only need **one function** from a package and don't want to load the whole thing

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
If you load the tidyverse with `library(tidyverse)`, you rarely need `::` notation within that ecosystem. But you will encounter it — and write it — more as your code grows.
:::

---

## How to Look Up a Function {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Inside Positron:**

- Type the function name and Positron shows a preview with argument hints
- The argument you are currently typing is **highlighted**
- `help(function_name)` or `?function_name` opens the full documentation pane

```r
# These are equivalent
help(pivot_longer)
?pivot_longer
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Online:**

- Tidyverse functions have excellent reference pages with worked examples: [tidyverse.org](https://www.tidyverse.org/)
- [R for Data Science (2e)](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/) is free online and is the textbook behind this course
- Stack Overflow for specific errors

:::
::::::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
**Habit to build:** Before Googling, try `?function_name` first. The Examples section at the bottom of every help file is often the fastest way to understand how something works.
:::

---

## A Note on Pipes {.smaller}

The pipe `|>` works because tidyverse functions share one convention: **the data frame is always the first argument**.

```r
gapminder |> filter(year == 2007)
# is the same as:
filter(gapminder, year == 2007)
```

. . .

This means piping works for most tidyverse functions — and many others — but **not universally**. You will encounter base R functions that expect a vector, not a data frame, as their first argument.

. . .

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**This works:**
```r
gapminder |>
  pull(lifeExp) |>   # extracts a vector
  mean()
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**This does not:**
```r
# mean() expects a vector, not a tibble
gapminder |> mean()  # Error
```

:::
::::::

. . .

`pull()` extracts a single column as a vector — useful when you need to pipe into a base R function.

# Part I: Tidyverse Follow-Up

---

## Why `summarise()` Exists {.smaller}

Last week you used `mean()`, `median()`, and similar functions inside `summarise()`. Here is why that pattern is necessary.

. . .

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Base R functions expect vectors:**
```r
# Works on a vector
mean(gapminder$lifeExp)

# Does not work on a tibble
gapminder |> mean()  # Error
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**`summarise()` bridges tibbles and vector functions:**
```r
# Correct: summarise feeds each column
# as a vector to the function inside
gapminder |>
  summarise(
    mean_le = mean(lifeExp),
    sd_le   = sd(lifeExp)
  )
```

:::
::::::

. . .

`summarise()` takes a tibble in, applies functions column-by-column, and returns a tibble. It is the bridge between the tidyverse world and base R summary statistics.

---

## Controlling How Much Prints {.smaller}

Tibbles print only 10 rows by default. You can override this with `print()`:

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```r
# Print first 25 rows
gapminder |> print(n = 25)

# Print all rows
gapminder |> print(n = Inf)

# Print specific columns wide
gapminder |> print(width = Inf)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

This is purely for **interactive inspection** — do not put `print(n = Inf)` in a document you are rendering. Rendered output should show only what your reader needs to see, controlled with `head()` or careful selection.

:::
::::::

# Part II: Reshaping Data

---

## Wide vs. Long -- A Reminder {.smaller}

Recall from last week: tidy data means one variable per column, one observation per row.

**Wide format** (common in spreadsheets, Excel exports, Eurostat downloads):

| country | ghg_2020 | ghg_2021 | ghg_2022 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Germany | 9.0 | 9.4 | 9.3 |
| France | 5.6 | 5.9 | 5.7 |

. . .

**Long (tidy) format** (what the tidyverse expects):

| country | year | ghg |
|---|---|---|
| Germany | 2020 | 9.0 |
| Germany | 2021 | 9.4 |
| France | 2020 | 5.6 |
| France | 2021 | 5.9 |

. . .

> In the wide version, `year` is hiding inside column names. `ggplot2` and `dplyr` cannot see it there. You must reshape first.

---

## `pivot_longer()` -- Wide to Long {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(tidyverse)

ghg_wide |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -country, # all columns except country
    names_to = "year", # new column for old headers
    values_to = "ghg" # new column for values
  )
```

Key arguments:

- `cols` — which columns to reshape (everything that is *not* an ID variable)
- `names_to` — what to call the new column that holds the old column names
- `values_to` — what to call the new column that holds the values

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Before:**

| country | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Germany | 9.0 | 9.4 | 9.3 |
| France | 5.6 | 5.9 | 5.7 |

**After:**

| country | year | ghg |
|---|---|---|
| Germany | 2020 | 9.0 |
| Germany | 2021 | 9.4 |
| Germany | 2022 | 9.3 |
| France | 2020 | 5.6 |
| France | 2021 | 5.9 |
| France | 2022 | 5.7 |

:::
::::::

---

## `pivot_wider()` -- Long to Wide {.smaller}

The reverse: sometimes you need wide format for a table or for a specific model.

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
ghg_long |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = year, # column whose values become headers
    values_from = ghg # column whose values fill the cells
  )
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Before:**

| country | year | ghg |
|---|---|---|
| Germany | 2020 | 9.0 |
| Germany | 2021 | 9.4 |
| France | 2020 | 5.6 |

**After:**

| country | 2020 | 2021 |
|---|---|---|
| Germany | 9.0 | 9.4 |
| France | 5.6 | NA |

:::
::::::

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
`pivot_longer()` is far more common in practice. Most raw data arrives wide; tidy analysis requires long.
:::

---

## Practical Example: What Happens When You Load Eurostat Data {.smaller}

Let's see what actually happens when we naively load the GHG file:

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true
# First attempt — no skip
ghg_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx",
  sheet = "Sheet 1"
)

names(ghg_raw)
```

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

::: {.callout-warning}
**What went wrong?** The file has 9 rows of metadata before the actual data begins. `read_excel()` read the first row as column headers — giving us nonsense names like `...2`, `...4`, `Tonnes per capita`.

This is extremely common with official statistical releases (Eurostat, World Bank, UN). **Always inspect your file before loading.**
:::

:::
::::::

---

## Step 1: Skip the Metadata Rows {.smaller}

We know from inspecting the file that the real column headers (`TIME`, `2019`, ...) are on row 10. So we skip 9 rows:

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true
ghg_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx",
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  skip = 9 # rows 1–9 are metadata
)

ghg_raw
```

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

Better — but notice:

- The first column is named `TIME` (we want `country`)
- Column names `2019`, `2020`, ... start with a number
- There are stray footer rows at the bottom (`Special value`, `:`)

::: {.callout-tip}
**How to find the right `skip` value:** Open the file in Excel and count how many rows appear before the header row. Subtract 1 — that is your `skip` value.
:::

:::
::::::

---

## Step 2: `janitor::clean_names()` to the Rescue {.smaller}

The `janitor` package's `clean_names()` function converts all column names to safe, consistent snake_case automatically:

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true
ghg_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx",
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  skip = 9
) |>
  clean_names()

names(ghg_raw)
```

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

`clean_names()` converts `2019` → `x2019`, `2020` → `x2020`, etc. — adding an `x` prefix to columns that would otherwise start with a number.

This removes the backtick problem entirely. `x2019` is a legal R column name that needs no special quoting.

::: {.callout-note}
`clean_names()` also handles spaces, dashes, parentheses, and accented characters. Make it a habit to call it immediately after every `read_excel()` or `read_csv()`.
:::

:::
::::::

---

## Step 3: Rename, Filter, Drop, Pivot {.smaller}

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true
ghg_clean <- ghg_raw |>
  rename(country = time) |> # TIME → country
  filter(!is.na(country), !country %in% c("Special value", ":")) |> # remove footer rows
  select(-x2019) |> # drop 2019 (not in GDP data)
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -country,
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "ghg_pc"
  ) |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(str_remove(year, "^x"))) # "x2020" → 2020

ghg_clean
```

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
`str_remove(year, "^x")` strips the leading `x` that `clean_names()` added, then `as.integer()` converts the result to a number. The `^` means "start of string" — a small taste of regular expressions, which we will return to later.
:::

---

## Why This Matters for Pivoting {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Before (wide, after clean_names):**

| country | x2020 | x2021 | x2022 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Germany | 9.0 | 9.4 | 9.3 |
| France | 5.6 | 5.9 | 5.7 |

Without `clean_names()`, the year columns would be named `2020`, `2021` — requiring backticks everywhere. With `clean_names()`, they become `x2020`, `x2021`, which R handles without complaint.

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**After (long / tidy):**

| country | year | ghg_pc |
|---|---|---|
| Germany | 2020 | 9.0 |
| Germany | 2021 | 9.4 |
| Germany | 2022 | 9.3 |
| France | 2020 | 5.6 |

Once pivoted, `year` is just a regular variable column. No backticks, no special treatment needed.

::: {.callout-note}
**The backtick trap** still exists in R — column names starting with numbers or containing spaces must be wrapped in backticks. `clean_names()` is the easiest way to avoid it entirely.
:::

:::
::::::

# Part III: Joining Data

---

## Why Join? {.smaller}

Real research rarely lives in a single dataset. You might have:

- GHG emissions from Eurostat in one file
- GDP per capita from Eurostat in another file
- EU accession dates from Wikipedia
- Country codes from a reference package

. . .

**Joining** combines these into a single analysis-ready dataset by matching rows on a shared key — usually a country name, country code, or year.

---

## The Four Main Joins {.smaller}

All joins take two tables and a **key** (the column(s) to match on).

| Join | What it keeps |
|---|---|
| `left_join(x, y)` | All rows from `x`; matching rows from `y`; `NA` where no match |
| `right_join(x, y)` | All rows from `y`; matching rows from `x`; `NA` where no match |
| `inner_join(x, y)` | Only rows that match **in both** tables |
| `full_join(x, y)` | All rows from both; `NA` wherever there is no match |

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
**In practice, `left_join()` is by far the most common.** Start with your main analysis dataset on the left, and add variables from auxiliary datasets on the right. Rows in your main dataset are always preserved.
:::

---

## `left_join()` -- An Example {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Both datasets are now tidy long format
# with matching columns: country + year

eu_data <- ghg |>
  left_join(gdp, by = c("country", "year"))
```

The `by` argument names the **key columns** — the columns that must match for two rows to be joined.

. . .

If the key column has different names in the two tables:

```r
left_join(x, y, by = c("country" = "nation"))
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

After joining, always verify the result:

```r
# Check dimensions make sense
nrow(eu_data)
glimpse(eu_data)

# Check for unexpected NAs introduced by the join
eu_data |>
  filter(is.na(gdp_pc)) |>
  distinct(country, year)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
NAs after a `left_join` usually mean one of two things: the key didn't match (e.g. a country name is spelled differently across datasets), or the right-hand dataset simply has no data for that row. Always investigate before proceeding.
:::

:::
::::::

---

## Visualising the Joins {.smaller}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
# x and y are created in the setup chunk
```

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**`x` (GHG data):**
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
x
```

**`y` (GDP data):**
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
y
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**`left_join(x, y)`** -- keeps all of `x`:
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
left_join(x, y, by = "country")
```

**`inner_join(x, y)`** -- only matches:
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
inner_join(x, y, by = "country")
```

:::
::::::

# Part IV: Missing Values

---

## NA in R {.smaller}

In R, missing values are represented as `NA` — *not available*.

```{r}
#| eval: false
x <- c(1, 2, NA, 4)

is.na(x) # TRUE for each NA position
sum(is.na(x)) # count of NAs
mean(x) # NA -- most functions propagate NA by default
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) # 2.33 -- explicitly remove NAs
```

. . .

- **`NA` is not zero.** It means the value is unknown.
- **`NA` is contagious.** Any arithmetic involving `NA` returns `NA`.
- **`na.rm = TRUE`** is the standard argument to ignore NAs in summary functions.

---

## Finding and Filtering Missing Values {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Rows where a variable IS missing
data |>
  filter(is.na(gdp_pc))

# Rows where a variable is NOT missing
data |>
  filter(!is.na(gdp_pc))

# Count NAs per column
data |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.))))
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Remove rows with any NA
data |> drop_na()

# Remove rows with NA in specific column
data |> drop_na(gdp_pc)
```

::: {.callout-warning}
**`drop_na()` silently removes data.** Before using it, check *how many* rows it would remove and ask whether those missing values are informative. A country that never reported GHG emissions is not the same as a country with a data entry error.
:::

:::
::::::

---

## The Problem with Imputation {.smaller}

A common temptation: replace `NA` with the column mean.

```{r}
#| eval: false
data |>
  mutate(gdp_pc = ifelse(is.na(gdp_pc), mean(gdp_pc, na.rm = TRUE), gdp_pc))
```

. . .

**What assumption does this imply?**

- You are asserting that the missing country's GDP is *average* — but you have no evidence of that.
- Mean imputation **compresses variance** and **distorts correlations**.
- It treats missingness as random, which is rarely true. Poor countries may be less likely to report data.

. . .

::: {.callout-important}
**There is no free lunch with missing data.** Every approach — dropping, imputing, or modelling — makes an assumption. The correct response is to be **transparent**: report how many observations are missing and why you handled them the way you did. Never silently drop or impute without acknowledgment.
:::

---

## Coding Missings on Import {.smaller}

Many datasets use special codes for missing values: `-99`, `999`, `"n/a"`, `"."` (Stata).

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Tell readr which strings to treat as NA
my_data <- read_csv(
  "data/file.csv",
  na = c("", "NA", "-99", "n/a", ".")
)

# For Stata files, haven handles this automatically
library(haven)
my_data <- read_dta("data/file.dta")

# After loading, check for suspicious values
summary(my_data)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
**Always open your raw data file before loading it in R.** A two-minute inspection in Excel or a text editor tells you the structure, encoding, missing value codes, and any metadata rows — exactly the information you need to load it correctly.
:::

# Part V: Joining the Eurostat Data -- Full Pipeline

---

## End-to-End: Import, Clean, Reshape, Join {.smaller}

The complete pipeline — this is what your homework setup code will look like:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(janitor)

# --- GHG: skip 9 metadata rows, clean names, pivot ---
ghg <- read_excel("data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx", sheet = "Sheet 1", skip = 9) |>
  clean_names() |>
  rename(country = time) |>
  filter(!is.na(country), !country %in% c("Special value", ":")) |>
  select(-x2019) |>
  pivot_longer(-country, names_to = "year", values_to = "ghg_pc") |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(str_remove(year, "^x")))

# --- GDP: skip 8 metadata rows, clean names, pivot ---
gdp <- read_excel("data/Eurostat_GDPpc.xlsx", sheet = "Sheet 1", skip = 8) |>
  clean_names() |>
  rename(country = time) |>
  filter(!is.na(country)) |>
  pivot_longer(-country, names_to = "year", values_to = "gdp_pc") |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(str_remove(year, "^x")))

# --- Join on country + year ---
eu_data <- left_join(ghg, gdp, by = c("country", "year"))
```

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false
glimpse(eu_data)
```

---

## Cleaning Country Names & Factors {.smaller}

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
eu_data <- eu_data |>
  mutate(
    country = fct(country),
    country = fct_recode(
      country,
      "EU27" = "European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)"
    )
  )
```

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false
eu_data |>
  count(country) |>
  print(n = 5)
```

. . .

**Why factors?** Factors store a fixed set of categories. They are essential for correct plot ordering, legend display, and later for regression dummy coding. `fct_recode()` renames specific levels without touching the others.

# Part VI: ggplot2 Deep Dive

---

## Recap: The Three Layers {.smaller}

Every ggplot is assembled from the same building blocks:

```r
ggplot(data, aes(x = ..., y = ..., color = ...)) +
  geom_*() +
  scale_*() +
  labs() +
  theme_*()
```

. . .

| Layer | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `ggplot()` + `aes()` | Canvas: data and variable-to-visual mappings |
| `geom_*()` | What to draw: points, lines, bars, boxes, text, ... |
| `scale_*()` | How to map: axis limits, colors, log transforms, ... |
| `labs()` | Text: title, subtitle, axis labels, captions, legend title |
| `theme_*()` | Appearance: fonts, gridlines, background, ... |

---

## The Main Aesthetics {.smaller}

`aes()` maps **variables** to **visual properties**. The main ones:

| Aesthetic | What it controls |
|---|---|
| `x`, `y` | Position on axes |
| `color` | Outline / line / point color |
| `fill` | Interior fill color (bars, boxes, ribbons) |
| `alpha` | Transparency (0 = invisible, 1 = opaque) |
| `size` | Point size or line thickness |
| `shape` | Point shape (circle, triangle, square, ...) |
| `linetype` | Line style (solid, dashed, dotted, ...) |
| `label` | Text label (used with `geom_text()`) |
| `group` | Grouping without visual distinction |

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
**Inside `aes()` = mapped to data. Outside `aes()` = fixed constant.**
`geom_point(color = "red")` makes all points red.  
`geom_point(aes(color = continent))` colors by the `continent` variable.
:::

---

## Key Geoms {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

| Geom | Use case |
|---|---|
| `geom_point()` | Scatterplot |
| `geom_line()` | Time series, trends |
| `geom_col()` | Bar chart (uses your values) |
| `geom_bar()` | Bar chart (counts rows) |
| `geom_boxplot()` | Distribution summary |
| `geom_histogram()` | Distribution of one variable |
| `geom_density()` | Smoothed distribution |
| `geom_smooth()` | Trend / regression line |
| `geom_text()` | Text labels at data positions |
| `geom_hline()` / `geom_vline()` | Reference lines |

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

Geoms can be **layered**:

```r
ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
```

And **combined with group aesthetics**:

```r
ggplot(data, aes(x = year,
                 y = value,
                 color = country)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point()
```

:::
::::::

---

## Scales {.smaller}

Scales control how data values translate into visual properties.

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Axis scales:**
```r
scale_x_log10()        # log transform x axis
scale_y_continuous(
  limits = c(0, 20),   # axis range
  breaks = seq(0, 20, 5) # tick marks
)
scale_x_discrete()     # for factor axes
```

**Color scales:**
```r
scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1")
scale_color_manual(values = c("red", "blue"))
scale_fill_viridis_d()  # colorblind-friendly
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Why scales matter:**

- A log scale compresses a skewed distribution, revealing patterns hidden on a linear scale
- Color choice affects readability and accessibility — colorblind viewers cannot distinguish red and green
- `viridis` palettes are colorblind-safe and print well in grayscale

::: {.callout-tip}
For color-coded plots, always ask: *would this still be readable printed in black and white, or by someone with red-green color blindness?*
:::

:::
::::::

---

## Labels and Titles {.smaller}

```r
labs(
  title    = "GHG Emissions and GDP per Capita, EU Member States",
  subtitle = "Data: Eurostat, 2020–2022",
  x        = "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)",
  y        = "GHG Emissions per Capita (tonnes)",
  color    = "Membership",
  caption  = "Note: EU27 average excluded."
)
```

. . .

| Element | Guideline |
|---|---|
| **Title** | Describe what the plot shows, not what you conclude |
| **Subtitle** | Source, year, or key qualification |
| **Axis labels** | Always include units. "GDP" is ambiguous; "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)" is not |
| **Caption** | Data source, methodological notes, exclusions |
| **Legend title** | Rename via `color =`, `fill =` etc. in `labs()` |

---

## Themes {.smaller}

Themes control the non-data appearance of the plot.

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Built-in themes:**
```r
theme_bw()       # white background, grey gridlines
theme_minimal()  # minimal gridlines, no border
theme_classic()  # white background, no gridlines
theme_void()     # blank canvas (for maps, diagrams)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Customising a theme:**
```r
theme_bw() +
theme(
  plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
  axis.title    = element_text(size = 11),
  legend.position = "bottom",
  panel.grid.minor = element_blank()  # remove minor gridlines
)
```

:::
::::::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
For academic work, `theme_bw()` or `theme_minimal()` are good defaults. Avoid dark backgrounds for documents that will be printed. Set `legend.position = "bottom"` for plots with many categories — it preserves horizontal space.
:::

---

## Example: Scatterplot with Regression Line {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{r}
#| label: fig-scatter-eu
#| fig-cap: "GDP and GHG emissions, EU member states 2022"
#| eval: true
#| echo: true

eu_data |>
  filter(year == 2022, country != "EU27") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_pc, y = ghg_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "steelblue") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)",
    y = "GHG Emissions per Capita (tonnes)",
    title = "Wealth and Emissions in the EU, 2022",
    caption = "Source: Eurostat"
  )
```

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

Key elements:

- `geom_smooth(method = "lm")` adds a linear regression line with a confidence band (`se = TRUE`)
- `alpha = 0.7` reduces overplotting
- `size = 2` makes points more visible
- `country != "EU27"` excludes the aggregate from the point cloud

The positive slope suggests richer EU member states tend to emit more GHG per capita — though with substantial variation.

:::
::::::

---

## Example: Bar Chart with Custom Fill {.smaller}

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{r}
#| label: fig-bar-eu
#| fig-cap: "GHG emissions per capita by EU member state, 2022"
#| eval: true
#| fig-height: 5

eu_data |>
  filter(year == 2022) |>
  mutate(
    is_eu27 = country == "EU27",
    country = fct_reorder(country, ghg_pc)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = country, y = ghg_pc, fill = is_eu27)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "steelblue", "TRUE" = "firebrick"),
    labels = c("Member State", "EU27 Avg"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "GHG per Capita (tonnes)",
    title = "GHG Emissions by EU Member State, 2022",
    caption = "Source: Eurostat"
  )
```

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

Key elements:

- `fct_reorder(country, ghg_pc)` sorts countries by emission value
- `fill = is_eu27` maps a logical variable to color
- `scale_fill_manual()` assigns specific colors and renames the legend
- `coord_flip()` rotates the chart — always do this for many categories on the x-axis

:::
::::::

---

## Facets {.smaller}

`facet_wrap()` splits a plot into small multiples by a grouping variable.

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{r}
#| label: fig-facet-eu
#| fig-cap: "GDP vs. GHG by year, EU member states"
#| eval: true
#| fig-height: 4

eu_data |>
  filter(country != "EU27") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_pc, y = ghg_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~year) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)",
    y = "GHG per Capita (tonnes)",
    caption = "Source: Eurostat"
  )
```

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

`facet_wrap(~ year)` creates one panel per year.

`facet_grid(row ~ col)` for a two-dimensional grid.

**Free vs. fixed scales:**
```r
# Each panel uses the same axes (default)
facet_wrap(~ year)

# Each panel scales to its own data
facet_wrap(~ year, scales = "free")
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Free scales make within-panel patterns clearer, but make **cross-panel comparison impossible**. Be explicit in your figure caption about which you are using and why.
:::

:::
::::::

# Part VII: Workflow Practices

---

## The `.gitignore` File {.smaller}

A `.gitignore` file tells Git which files to **never track**. Place it in the root of your repository.

```
# .gitignore

# R temporary files
.Rhistory
.RData
.Rproj.user/

# Rendered outputs (optional -- only if you regenerate from source)
*.pdf
*.html

# Large data files (if you store data elsewhere)
data/*.csv
data/*.xlsx

# OS files
.DS_Store        # Mac
Thumbs.db        # Windows
```

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
**Never commit sensitive data** (passwords, API keys, personal data) to a Git repository. Even private repositories can be accidentally made public. Add such files to `.gitignore` immediately.
:::

---

## Analytical Problem-Solving {.smaller}

When you face a data analysis task, **divide and conquer**.

. . .

**Example: How do EU member states' GHG emissions relate to their wealth?**

. . .

Break this into sub-problems:

1. How do I load the GHG and GDP data into R? *(import)*
2. The data is wide — how do I reshape it? *(pivot)*
3. The country names differ slightly — how do I merge? *(join)*
4. I need a new column for membership status *(mutate)*
5. How do I visualise the relationship? *(ggplot)*
6. Each of these can be broken down further...

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
After each sub-problem:
- **Test in the console** — does the intermediate result look right?
- **Render your document** — does it still compile?
- **Commit to Git** — you have a working checkpoint to fall back to
:::

---

## A Practical Workflow Loop {.smaller}

```
1. Set up .qmd → render once → confirm it works
2. Write a small chunk → run in console → check result
3. When chunk is correct → render full document
4. Commit to Git with a clear message: "add data import step"
5. Repeat
```

. . .

**Why render frequently?** Problems compound. A chunk that works in isolation may fail in a rendered document because of:

- Missing `library()` calls
- Objects that exist in your environment but are not created in the document
- Chunk execution order dependencies

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
**Rule of thumb:** If you would be upset to lose the last 20 minutes of work, commit it. Commits are free.
:::

# Useful Extras

---

## Inline Code in Quarto {.smaller}

You can embed R results directly in prose using backtick notation:

```markdown
The dataset contains `r nrow(eu_data)` observations across
`r n_distinct(eu_data$country)` countries.
The mean GHG per capita in 2022 was
`r eu_data |> filter(year == 2022) |> pull(ghg_pc) |> mean(na.rm = TRUE) |> round(1)` tonnes.
```

. . .

Renders as:

> The dataset contains **84** observations across **28** countries. The mean GHG per capita in 2022 was **7.9** tonnes.

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
Inline code means your text and your numbers **never get out of sync**. When the data changes, the prose updates automatically on the next render. Use this for all specific numbers you report.
:::

---

## `janitor`: Beyond `clean_names()` {.smaller}

You already used `clean_names()` in the import pipeline. The `janitor` package has more useful tools:

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**`tabyl()` — a tidy cross-tabulation:**

```{r}
#| eval: true
# Frequency table for one variable
eu_data |>
  tabyl(year)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**`tabyl()` with two variables:**

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Cross-tab: country × year
eu_data |>
  tabyl(country, year)
```

`tabyl()` returns a tibble — unlike base R's `table()`, the result can be piped further and works cleanly in Quarto documents.

::: {.callout-tip}
Use `tabyl()` whenever you want a quick count or cross-tabulation in a rendered document. It is cleaner than `count()` for two-way tables and integrates with `kable()` directly.
:::

:::
::::::

---

## `kable()` for Printed Tables {.smaller}

For tables in rendered documents, `knitr::kable()` produces clean output in both HTML and PDF:

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true
eu_data |>
  filter(year == 2022, country != "EU27") |>
  slice_max(ghg_pc, n = 5) |>
  select(country, ghg_pc, gdp_pc) |>
  kable(
    col.names = c("Country", "GHG pc (tonnes)", "GDP pc (EU27=100)"),
    digits = 1,
    caption = "Top 5 emitters in 2022"
  )
```

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

`kable()` arguments:

- `col.names` — rename columns in the output
- `digits` — round numbers
- `caption` — table caption (cross-referenceable with `tbl-` label)
- `format = "latex"` / `"html"` — rarely needed, Quarto detects format automatically

For more control: the `kableExtra` package adds column alignment, row grouping, and color highlighting.

:::
::::::

# Time to Practice!

---

## Exercise Overview

- Clone the Week 5 exercise repo from GitHub
- Open `exercise_week05.qmd` in Positron
- You will work with the Eurostat GHG and GDP datasets
- Tasks follow the arc of today's session: **import → reshape → join → visualise**
- **Fast finishers:** optional extension tasks at the bottom

::: notes
Show import live: load one file, demonstrate the skip argument, show what happens without skip. Then pivot, then join. Let them work on the rest. Reserve 10 min for homework preview.
:::

---

## Homework

- Distributed via GitHub (same workflow as previous weeks)
- Submit a **rendered Quarto document** (`.qmd` + rendered PDF)
- The PDF should show **only plots and prose** — no code
- **Due:** one week from today

You will work with the same Eurostat datasets to produce five publication-quality plots. The homework will ask you to combine everything from today: import with `readxl`, reshape with `pivot_longer`, join with `left_join`, and visualise with `ggplot2`.

---

## Homework Preview: What You Will Produce {.smaller}

1. **Boxplot** — distribution of GHG emissions per capita across years (excluding EU27)
2. **Bar chart** — GHG emissions in 2022, countries ordered by emissions, EU27 highlighted
3. **Scatterplot** — GDP vs. GHG, colored by EU membership era (pre/post 2000 accession), with regression line
4. **Labelled scatterplot** — country ISO codes as labels instead of points, faceted by membership era
5. **Labelled scatterplot with free scales** — same as above, but each panel scales independently

. . .

For each plot: clear labels, titles, captions, and 1–3 sentences of interpretation.

Final question: *Why might free scales in Plot 5 be problematic?*

# Thank you for your attention and see you next week!

<a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H2a2 2 0 0 1-2-2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a>
